text: This video will demonstrate the benefit of robotic ETEP incisional hernia repair in the patient with a petite torso. Traditionally, minimally invasive surgery has been very challenging in petite torso. This case is interesting in that the defect size was relatively large for the patient's size. She was four foot eleven and a hundred twenty-five pounds. The defect is nine centimeters wide by fifteen centimeters long. Traditionally, transabdominal minimally invasive surgery, whether it's laparoscopic or robotic, is very frustrating at best, but usually is not possible due to the inability for proper port spacing and achieving adequate workspace to secure a large piece of mesh.

This case, I believe, was possible due to the benefits of both robotics and the ETIP reef stopper operation. Next, you'll see the CT scan. The patient's rectus width was only four point four centimeters. On CT, the measurement of the defect width was eight point five centimeters. Intra-op, it was about nine centimeters. The sagittal view shows you that this defect was about fourteen to fifteen centimeters long. You can see this takes up about half of her abdominal length. The benefits of ETEP include the ability to place ports more medial. It's usually medial to the linea similaris, which means that there's more room for proper port spacing, since the distance between the cossum margin and the iliac crest allows for adequate port spacing. The mesh is also sandwiched in the retro-rectus space. Rather than needing to be tacked with trans-fascials or tackers, the mesh is just sandwiched in a space and left in there without the need for securing with sutures or tacks. And then because it's in the retro-rectus space, you can place it underneath the ports and provide wide mesh overlap for the defect. The robot really makes the ETEP operation shine.

The surgeon begins the operation with optical entry in the right upper quadrant, placing three ports medial to the right linea semilunaris. The port insertion is challenging due to the small workspace, particularly in the right lower quadrant. The robotic instruments are docked, allowing for precise micro-movements in the tight lower abdominal space to avoid injury to surrounding structures.

The surgeon begins the procedure in the upper abdomen, where there is more space, and dissects the retro-rectus space. The crossover is initiated, and the surgeon enters the abdominal cavity due to minimal preperitoneal fat around the hernia. In the upper abdomen, a plane is established in the pre-peritoneal space, and the surgeon brings down the fat. The surgeon is now intra-abdominal, taking down adhesions and addressing the pseudosac to manage the anticipated posterior gap.

The surgeon completes the crossover in the lower abdomen by cutting the patient's right posterior rectal sheath to access the preperitoneal space below the umbilicus. The tight working space is visible as adhesions within the hernia sac are lysed. The surgeon enters the left retroactive space both below and above the hernia defect, using a grasper to guide the incision in the posterior rectus sheath. The retro-rectus dissection is completed, and the round ligament in the left pelvis is divided. Due to the large defect size, a bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) is initiated at the arcuate line to achieve sufficient anterior release for defect closure without tension.

The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum from behind the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring not to pull the anterior lamella of the internal oblique into the transection line. The upper TAR (transversus abdominis release) is initiated under the diaphragm, dividing the posterior rectus sheath, the posterior lamella of the internal oblique, transverse abdominis, and the diaphragm's posterior contribution. The dissection continues to connect the upper and lower TAR, revealing the defect measuring approximately nine by fifteen centimeters. The surgeon notes difficulty in visualizing the ipsilateral linea alba while closing the posterior layer in a confined workspace.

The surgeon uses the pseudo-sac held in the left hand to patch a large defect, as the posterior sheath edges cannot be brought together despite a left-sided TAR. The pseudo-sac effectively fills the gap, avoiding the need for alternative patches. A three-hole V-lock, 180 suture is used to close the final defect in the posterior gap. The surgeon plans to close the anterior defect by addressing the diastasis and repositioning the camera for a top-down view.

The surgeon shifts the instruments to the left upper quadrant port, providing a clear view of the edge of the mesh. Image aversion is used to facilitate suturing, allowing the left hand to function as the right hand. The right hand easily reaches necessary areas, while the left hand is constrained by the proximity of the port. The large piece of mesh is placed to cover the entire space, extending beyond the area of the transversus abdominis release (TAR) to cover the left linea semilunaris. No drains are placed.
